what-the-terminal-is.label: What the Terminal Is
what-the-terminal-is.summary: Type simple commands, create small files, and see the workspace respond.

sketch0.title: The terminal is a conversation with the computer

sketch1.title: The prompt waits for your next command

ci-create-linux-start.title: Create a welcome folder
ci-create-linux-start.prompt: Set up a quick welcome area for your first terminal session: create `welcome/identity.txt`.
ci-create-linux-start.hint: Use `mkdir -p welcome`, then create the file inside it.

help.concept: Use the terminal to change the workspace, then check the result with `ls`.
help.hint_1: Use `mkdir -p welcome`, then create the file inside it.
help.hint_2: Submit when the required path exists and any unwanted path is gone.

ci-make-command-practice.title: Save a command note
ci-make-command-practice.prompt: Add a place to remember useful commands: create `welcome/commands.txt`.
ci-make-command-practice.hint: Create the folder first if it is not there, then use `touch` or redirect text into the file.

help.concept: Use the terminal to change the workspace, then check the result with `ls`.
help.hint_1: Create the folder first if it is not there, then use `touch` or redirect text into the file.
help.hint_2: Submit when the required path exists and any unwanted path is gone.

ci-create-first-lab.title: Mark today’s check-in
ci-create-first-lab.prompt: Create `daily-checkin/ready.txt` to mark that the workspace is ready.
ci-create-first-lab.hint: A nested path works well with `mkdir -p` plus `touch`.

terminal-is-conversation.title: The terminal listens for one command at a time
terminal-is-conversation.bodyMarkdown:
A terminal is a direct way to ask Linux to do work. You type a command, press Enter, and read the response.

Start with commands that answer simple questions:

```bash
whoami
pwd
ls
```

Then use small changes, such as creating a folder or a note, so you can see the result in the workspace.

prompt-and-command.title: The prompt is your starting line
prompt-and-command.bodyMarkdown:
The prompt marks where the next command goes. A command can print information, create a folder, or create a file.

Safe first commands:

```bash
pwd
ls
mkdir welcome
touch welcome/identity.txt
```

Use `ls` often. It tells you what changed.

safe-first-habits.title: Small commands are easier to trust
safe-first-habits.bodyMarkdown:
Good terminal habits start with checking before changing. Look around with `pwd` and `ls`, then create one clear thing at a time.

For now, stay inside the lesson workspace and avoid wide delete commands. You will practice removal later with small files that are safe to throw away.
